questions.q: Nur Jahan was the influential consort of which Mughal emperor?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan was the chief queen of the fourth Mughal emperor, Jahangir.

questions.q: In which century did Nur Jahan wield her great political power?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan exercised her remarkable influence in the early 17th century during Jahangir's reign.

questions.q: What was remarkable about the coins of the Mughal Empire during Nur Jahan's ascendancy?
options: They were issued in her name
They were made of pure gold only
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan had coins struck in her own name, a rare and striking sign of a Mughal woman's authority.

questions.q: The title 'Nur Jahan' is commonly translated as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Jahangir gave her the title Nur Jahan, meaning 'Light of the World'.

questions.q: Nur Jahan is best remembered for her role as which of the following?
options: The power behind the Mughal throne
A founder of a new dynasty
questions.explanation: She effectively guided court affairs, earning her the reputation as the power behind Jahangir's throne.

questions.q: What was Nur Jahan's original given name before her marriage to Jahangir?
questions.explanation: She was born Mehr-un-Nissa and later given the titles Nur Mahal and then Nur Jahan.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's family was of which origin?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan was of Persian descent; her father had migrated to India from Persia.

questions.q: Nur Jahan being a woman holding such power was unusual chiefly because of the customs of which court?
questions.explanation: Her open exercise of political power was a rare role for a woman within the Mughal court.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's niece, who married Jahangir's son Shah Jahan, is famous as which figure?
questions.explanation: Mumtaz Mahal, for whom the Taj Mahal was built, was Nur Jahan's niece.

questions.q: Nur Jahan rose to her position after becoming Jahangir's wife in which year?
questions.explanation: Mehr-un-Nissa married Jahangir in 1611, after which her influence grew rapidly.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's father, who served as Jahangir's chief minister, was given which title?
questions.explanation: Her father Mirza Ghiyas Beg held the title Itimad-ud-Daula and served as Jahangir's wazir.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's brother, an influential noble at court, was known by which title?
questions.explanation: Her brother Abul Hasan held the title Asaf Khan and was a leading figure at Jahangir's court.

questions.q: Before marrying Jahangir, Nur Jahan had been the wife of which man, a Mughal officer who held a posting in Bengal?
questions.explanation: Her first husband was Sher Afgan (Ali Quli Beg Istajlu), a Persian-born Mughal officer who died in Bengal in 1607.

questions.q: Nur Jahan built an exquisite marble tomb in Agra for her parents. For whom is it named?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan commissioned the Tomb of Itimad-ud-Daula, often called the 'Baby Taj', for her father and mother.

questions.q: Along with her father, brother, and Jahangir, Nur Jahan is often described as the leading member of what informal grouping?
options: The Nur Jahan junta or clique
questions.explanation: Historians describe a 'Nur Jahan junta' of her family that dominated Mughal politics in Jahangir's later reign.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's only daughter, born during her first marriage, was which person?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan's daughter Ladli Begum was later married to Jahangir's son Shahryar as part of her political maneuvering.

questions.q: In the succession struggle at the end of Jahangir's reign, Nur Jahan backed which prince against Shah Jahan?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan supported Prince Shahryar, her son-in-law, hoping to keep power, but Shah Jahan ultimately prevailed.

questions.q: When the rebel general Mahabat Khan seized Jahangir in 1626, Nur Jahan was credited with which action?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan is credited with outwitting Mahabat Khan and securing Jahangir's freedom after his 1626 coup.

questions.q: Contemporary accounts describe Nur Jahan as skilled in which pursuit, even hunting tigers?
questions.explanation: Sources praise Nur Jahan's skill with the gun, including accounts of her shooting tigers from a howdah.

questions.q: After Jahangir's death and Shah Jahan's accession, what became of Nur Jahan?
options: She lived in retirement on a pension at Lahore
questions.explanation: Sidelined by Shah Jahan, Nur Jahan lived quietly in Lahore on a pension until her death in 1645.

questions.q: Nur Jahan was also active as a patron of architecture and which other creative field?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan is associated with innovations in dress, perfumes, and textile design at the Mughal court.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's tomb is located in which city, near that of Jahangir?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan was buried at Shahdara in Lahore, close to the tomb of her husband Jahangir.

questions.q: Nur Jahan was first given which earlier title by Jahangir before being elevated to 'Nur Jahan'?
questions.explanation: Jahangir first titled her Nur Mahal ('Light of the Palace') and later Nur Jahan ('Light of the World').

questions.q: Imperial decrees (farmans) during Jahangir's later years sometimes bore which feature reflecting Nur Jahan's authority?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan's seal appeared on farmans, and her name was joined to the emperor's in official acts, signalling her power.

questions.q: Nur Jahan's father, Mirza Ghiyas Beg, came from a noble family of which country before entering Mughal service?
questions.explanation: Mirza Ghiyas Beg belonged to a Persian family (often traced to Tehran) that migrated from Safavid Persia and entered Akbar's service.

questions.q: It is recorded that Nur Jahan was born while her family was migrating, reportedly at which place?
questions.explanation: Tradition holds Mehr-un-Nissa was born around 1577 near Kandahar as her impoverished family journeyed to India.

questions.q: Nur Jahan held the senior harem rank of 'Padshah Begum', which made her what within the imperial household?
options: Chief queen and head of the harem
A regent for a minor king
questions.explanation: As Padshah Begum, Nur Jahan was the foremost lady of the Mughal household, reinforcing her political standing.

questions.q: The marble inlay technique seen in Itimad-ud-Daula's tomb, built by Nur Jahan, foreshadowed the decoration of which later monument?
questions.explanation: The delicate pietra dura inlay of Itimad-ud-Daula's tomb anticipated the style later perfected on the Taj Mahal.

questions.q: In the 1626 crisis, after rescuing Jahangir, Nur Jahan's influence was finally curbed by the rise of which prince to power?
questions.explanation: Prince Khurram's accession as Shah Jahan in 1628 ended Nur Jahan's political career.

questions.q: Nur Jahan died in which year, having outlived Jahangir by nearly two decades?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan died in 1645 in Lahore, about eighteen years after Jahangir's death in 1627.
